content.claim: The 2013 centralization reform (KLIK) that transferred municipal school governance and funding to state oversight aimed to equalize resources across municipalities but produced contested outcomes: resource constraints of smaller schools were addressed, but previously better-funded urban/metropolitan schools experienced funding decline, and the system was criticized as producing over-centralization, opacity, and systematic under-budgeting.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Education finance lens
limitations: Pro and con assessments come from advocacy positions rather than independent evaluation
Counterfactual (what would have occurred under continued decentralization) not observed
Subsequent re-decentralization to 58-60 district centres (2017) complicates attribution of outcomes to KLIK specifically
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
